epigraph.text:
A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a 
just weight is his delight.

content.text:
What is the "typical" value in a set of data? This seemingly simple 
question has multiple answers, each useful in different situations. The 
**mean**, **median**, and **mode** are three different 
ways to describe the center of a data set, and choosing the right one 
matters.

The **mean** is what most people call the "average." You find it 
by adding all the values and dividing by how many values there are.

content.title: Mean
content.content:
The **mean** of a data set is the sum of all values 
divided by the number of values:
\[
\text{Mean} = \frac{\text{Sum of all values}}{\text{Number of values}}
\]

content.title: Calculating the mean
content.problem: Find the mean of the test scores: 85, 92, 78, 88, 92.

content.title: Mean with decimals
content.problem:
A basketball player scored these points in six games: 15, 22, 18, 25, 
19, 23. Find the mean.
content.solution:
$$
\text{Mean} &= \frac{15 + 22 + 18 + 25 + 19 + 23}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{122}{6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 20.33
$$
The mean is approximately **20.3 points** per game.

content.text:
The **median** is the middle value when data is arranged in order. 
Half the values are below it, and half are above it.

content.title: Median
content.content:
The **median** is the middle value of a data set 
when the values are arranged in order.

- If there is an **odd** number of values, the median is 
 the middle value.

- If there is an **even** number of values, the median is 
 the mean of the two middle values.

content.title: Finding the Median
content.content:
1. Arrange the values in order from least to greatest.

2. Count the number of values ($n$).

3. If $n$ is odd, the median is the value in position 
 $\frac{n+1}{2}$.

4. If $n$ is even, the median is the average of the values in 
 positions $\frac{n}{2}$ and $\frac{n}{2} + 1$.

content.title: Median with odd number of values
content.solution:
**Step 1:** Arrange in order: 8, 11, 14, 15, 22

**Step 2:** There are 5 values (odd).

**Step 3:** The middle value is in position $\frac{5+1}{2} = 3$.

The median is the 3rd value: **14**.

content.title: Median with even number of values
content.solution:
**Step 1:** Arrange in order: 17, 19, 23, 28, 31, 35

**Step 2:** There are 6 values (even).

**Step 3:** The two middle values are in positions 3 and 4: 
23 and 28.

**Step 4:** Find their mean: $\frac{23 + 28}{2} = \frac{51}{2} = 25.5$

The median is **25.5**.

content.text:
The **mode** is the value that appears most frequently. Unlike 
mean and median, a data set can have no mode, one mode, or multiple 
modes.

content.title: Mode
content.content:
The **mode** is the value that occurs most often in 
a data set.

- If no value repeats, there is **no mode**.

- If two values tie for most frequent, the data is 
 **bimodal** (two modes).

- If more than two values tie, the data is **multimodal**.

content.title: Finding the mode
content.solution:
1. The value 7 appears three times (more than any other). 
 Mode = **7**.

2. No value repeats. There is **no mode**.

3. Both 3 and 5 appear twice. The data is bimodal: 
 Mode = **3 and 5**.

content.text: Each measure of center has strengths and weaknesses:

rows: Data has no extreme values; you want to use all values
Data has outliers or is skewed; you want a "typical" value
Data is categorical (colors, brands); you want the most common
May not exist; ignores most values

content.title: Choosing the best measure
content.solution:
Let's calculate all three:

**Mean:** 
$\frac{180{,}000 + 195{,}000 + 210{,}000 + 185{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000}{5} 
= \frac{3{,}270{,}000}{5} = \$654{,}000$

\textbf{Median:} Arranged in order: 180,000; 185,000; 195,000; 210,000; 
2,500,000. The middle value is \$195,000.

**Mode:** No value repeats, so there is no mode.

The mean (\$654,000) is misleading---it's higher than 4 of the 5 houses! 
The \$2.5 million mansion pulls the mean way up.

The **median (\$195,000)** is the best measure here because it 
isn't distorted by the extreme value.

content.title: Mode for categorical data
content.problem:
A survey asked 50 students their favorite color. Results: Blue (18), 
Red (12), Green (8), Purple (7), Other (5). What is the best measure 
of center?
content.solution:
You cannot calculate a mean or median for colors—they aren't numbers! 
The **mode** is the only option: **Blue** is the most popular 
color.

content.title: The Origin of ``Average''
content.content:
The word "average" comes from the Arabic word *awariya*, meaning 
"damaged goods." In medieval trade, when cargo was damaged at sea, the 
loss was distributed equally among all merchants—each paid an 
"average" share. This concept of equal distribution evolved into our 
mathematical average.

marginNotes.text:
Adding one thing to another to 
discover the scheme of things.

What has been will be again, what 
has been done will be done again.

marginNotes.author: John Tukey
marginNotes.text:
The best thing about being a statistician is 
that you get to play in everyone's backyard.
